text.title: I Went to Korea 🇰🇷 and Everything Got Weird 😆 (in the Best Way 💕)
text.u: I was mortified. So why naked? This
mystery man. Why is this? Because yeah,
it actually feels
[Music]
great. Hi, this is Kimmy Park from Kim
Kore. And today we are going to talk
about my first times in Korea because a
few years ago I had no idea what kimchi
was. I was not listening to K-pop. I had
no interest in Korea really. But
everything changed with one single trip.
So let me take you on the journey of my
first in Korea. From the first bite of
kimchi on the plane to getting naked
among stranger in Korea to finding my
Korean name and reappropriating my
Korean
[Music]
identity. So before 2016 and my first
trip to Korea, I had really no idea
about Korea. Like I knew that my mom was
from there and had been adopted, but I
grew up in France where Korea was not
popular at the time. And yeah, so I knew
very little. But when that friend went
to do a year abroad in Korea, I decided
it was time for me to go and in 2016 I
went on the plane and went to Korea for
the first time. My first biggest
impression of Korea was in the subway.
Actually, was the first word I learned
in Korea because I knew and when I
arrived, I couldn't pronounce them that
well at the time, but was actually the
first word I learned on the day of my
arrival in Korea. Anyway, I was very
impressed by how the diat the subway is
very clean. It's very calm, how people
are sleeping on it because in France, no
one sleeps in a train really because
otherwise they're going to steal your
stuff. And I had a lots of fun doing all
the tourist stuff like exploring
palaces, doing a tea ceremony, going
hiking in the mountain where we actually
met some military guys that were there
and I was like, why is the military in a
mountain? I don't know. But one of them
was called Kim cuz it was written on his
tag and I was like, oh my god, your name
is Kim like me. Uh because at the time I
actually also didn't know that Kim is a
super common last name um in Korea. And
that's when I realized that me being
called Kim and telling them, "Oh, my
name is Kim." And they're like, "Okay,
but when then what's your first name?" I
realized that mistake that my mom made
when I was born and she wanted to call
me by your Korean name. And so she
called me Kim because she thought that
was a first name. And yeah, but we'll
come back to my name later in number 10.
So stay tuned.
So yeah, great transportation, beautiful
palaces and nature, the city lights,
like it was the other thing like going
at night outside and all these tall
building with so many lights. And I
remember going especially in Maong to do
loads of shopping. I'd never done so
much shopping in my life. Everything was
so cheap. Like all the clothes were like
so cool and affordable. And yeah, I
really really had a blast. And I've been
back several times to Korea since. I've
lived in Korea as well. But yeah, that
first impression of calm, beauty,
business,
lights, and
shopping. Now, the other major thing
that happened to me on this trip and in
my life really is that during this first
trip to Korea, I've reconnected with my
adopted mother's family whom I found
only a few weeks before going on this
trip. It was not really planned, but it
happened. It's a story for another video
because it's way too long. We
reconnected. We met for the first time.
This is a reason why I continued to go
in Korea after this trip. Even though
they were still strangers for me, but we
were to become very close over the next
few trips and few meetings that
happened. And it was a wonderful moment
full of bittersweet emotions because we
had to talk about a lot of difficult
topics. But also there was this joy to
have found each other. And it took a few
years for us to really become close. But
yeah, like now they really are my family
and I love them so much. And um this is
why this whole Korean journey, including
this first journey, are so important to
[Music]
me. Now, the other thing I didn't
mention before was the food. So, Korean
food was a great mystery to me. I had no
idea what Korean food was, and I was not
very familiar with Asian food in
general. Like for me, Asian food was
Japanese
sushi. Yeah, I know. And my first
memorable encounter with Korean food was
actually on the plane from Europe to
Soul because Air France was serving a
Korean meal. So, I had a bin even though
I had no idea what it was at the time.
And there was a little red box next to
it, which I ate after finishing all the
rest of my food. And I ate it. I just
like opened it and took the whole thing
and put it in my mouth at once. And
after I was like, "Oh my god, what is
this? It tastes weird. It's spicy. It's
what is this? And at that and at that
time there was a lady who was sitting
next to me who was a Korean atuma. Like
she was a older Korean lady who was
actually a tour guide of a big group of
aas and ais like a big group of Korean
people that have been traveling in
Europe and she was she looked at me and
she was laughing cuz I was kind of
like and she was like you should have
eaten this with the BB pup like with the
rice. you don't eat this by itself and
it's called kimchi. It's a bantan you're
supposed to eat with the rest of your
food. I was like, okay, cool. Thank you.
Yeah, I was a little bit embarrassed
because I was already like so red from
the spiciness and from coughing and from
being surprised by the taste of that
food and then that lady turned to her
big group, started speaking with other
people of the group. I'm like, why is
she speaking with the foreigner? I I
couldn't understand Korean obviously at
the time, but she was she was pointing
at me and being like, "Kimchi, kimchi."
And I was like, "Oh my god, what did she
say?" So yeah, my very first time eating
Korean food and especially kimchi was
yes, a very memorable, slightly shameful
moment.
Talking of shameful moment, another
thing I've done for the first time when
I went to Korea for that first time was
getting naked among strangers. Now hold
on. I have said I am French and some
people have the misconception that
French people likes to be naked in parks
and at the beach and this is wrong and
false. Mm- There are nudist beaches. It
happens. There are some some places, but
it's really not the norm. like French
people are not as free as you guys think
and I don't normally get naked in front
of people um that easily. So why did I
get naked? Actually um it was a cultural
experience. Hear me out. Have you guys
heard of gingerbang? These are public
bath houses and it's very common in
Korea. So back in the day people would
go there to literally have a bath. Uh it
was a hygienic service. Nowadays, people
have bathroom in their houses, but they
still go to a gingerbang for fun, to
hang out, to scrub each other's back,
which I find very strange because you're
naked when you do that. If you ever go
to one of those, I would recommend to go
to one of those and to not be too
scared. I was mortified the first time
and the first 15 seconds were really
hard for me who is really not used to
this. But basically, men and women are
separated in the like wet area. by wet
area. This is where you have the actual
bath. This is where you have some sauna,
some steam rooms. You arrive, you take
off all your clothes, you shower, then
you go around the baths, etc. Um,
another fun thing you can do there
is get scrubbed. So, what happened is
also funny. So, the scrubs there happen
on tables, but you're all next to each
other, completely naked, and you have an
old lady in her underwear, black
underwear. Why are they in underwear? I
don't know. That literally scrubs your
entire skin off. Like, they get rid of
all the skin from your body by really
scrubbing so hard. And it feels great.
You feel like a new little snake. But
it's a weird experience because you are
right next to each other naked on those
tables with those
Korean in their underwear scrubbing the
hell out of you and obviously she has to
scrub the whole of your body. Like she
leaves no place
untouched. She also washes your hair
which is nicer. Um but she has to do the
front but also the back. And I couldn't
speak Korean at the time and that lady
didn't speak English. And so to turn me
around, she said something and I was
like, "What?" And didn't understand
obviously. So, so I was lying down like
this or rather like this. And she took
my ankle and she just went and then I
turned like a little fish because when
they scrub you like you're actually full
of soap and on that plastic table you
just like
slip really easily. So she turned me
around and continued her work. And my
final surprise with this was that I had
requested a massage which I expected to
be relaxing but no. She started by like
hitting me real hard to get the blood
flow moving. And I was like what is this
torture? It's both humiliation and pain.
But once you get out once you walk out
of this experience you feel uh brand new
with a new skin very soft like a baby
baby skin and full of vitality.
So, it was weird, but it was great.
Actually, I don't know if I'm a
masochist, but like, but now every time
I go to Korea, I go to Gingerbang and I
always get the scrub massage combo
because yeah, it actually feels great.
Once you get fine with this nakedness
business, it's actually pretty good. So,
highly
recommended. So, if scrubbing is
actually really part of Korean hygienic
life, something else that I took from
Korea for my very first trip that I was
not doing before, but then became very
obsessed and religious about in my
beauty regimen is sun cream. I told you
I grew up in Europe and in Europe,
especially in 2000, you wanted to be
thin and tanned. That's it. Maybe
blonde. I don't know. Was it just me?
Yeah. So me with my cousin, we would we
would lather ourselves in oil and
literally go at the beach and just wait
for us to turn
orange like Donald Trump. And we had no
idea how bad this was for our skin. And
when I went to Korea and I found that
people had really good skin in general,
like my my the women in my family, my my
halman, my cousin had such nice skin.
And I was like, what are they doing
differently? We have the same genetics.
What I realized is that they were very
serious about putting sun cream every
day. And I was like,
"Oh." And that's when I started applying
sunscreen 50 plus plus every day, even
in winter, even if it rains, even if I
stay in the house. And I actually think
that it did wonder for my skin. Like, I
think it keeps me looking young, doesn't
it?
[Music]
Talking of beautiful people,
um I just wanted to mention this thing
that happened to me the first time I was
in Korea was that I saw a celebrity in
the street. And I know there's loads of
Tik Tok that says like, "Oh my god, if
you're just in Korea, you just see um
actors and K-pop idols and just like
Korean celebrities in the street all the
time." And I don't know where these
people go because I live for an entire
year in Korea and in that entire year I
saw nobody, right? Um, I saw people at
concerts. I saw people at meetup events.
Yes, sure they are supposed to be there.
But in the street, no, not really. And I
wish I had seen someone, okay? Because
now I love Korean celebrities. But back
in 2016, I knew nobody. And that's when
I saw a celebrity.
So, I was walking in Inadon, which is a
place that's famous for having
traditional items, and it's a bit of a
tourist spot. And that's also where I
bought this
cup. Can you read what it says? It
says, I had bought it for my boyfriend
at the time, and now he's my husband.
So, I think we can all thank this
guy. And I was walking in the street
when I saw a big group of people
following this one guy. And I turned to
my friend, and I was like, "What's
happening?" and she's like, "Oh, it's a
Korean celebrity." And I was like, "Oh
my god, a Korean celebrity whom I don't
know." So, what did I do? I just snapped
a picture of the
guy. That's it. Everybody was taking
pictures. So, I just did like the
others. Don't judge me. You would have
done the same. Now, fast forward a few
years. I was watching a Kdrama called
Let's Eat. And who do I see? That guy.
Correct. His name is, let me Google
this. Dun. That guy whom I seen in this
show. And yeah, I was like, "Oh my god,
it's the guy I saw in the streets all
these years ago. I finally know who he
is." So I internally thank myself for
having taken that pictures all these
years ago. Otherwise, he would have
always stayed this mystery man, this
mystery celebrity man in the street in
Korea. Now talking of Kama, the first
one I watched was called Oh My Ghost.
Not Oh my god, My Ghost. And I started
Kidramama when I was in Korea actually,
even though I only watched it on
Netflix. But next week's Korea because I
was there in August 2018. And if you
have never been in Korea in August, it's
actually really really hot and sticky
cuz it's the rainy season. So it's
raining all the time, but warm. So I was
just chilling at home and not really
knowing what to do. And I just started
to randomly watch this really weird
kidda which is a story of a girl who
died a virgin and who's not happy about
that.
And so she takes the body of another
girl who works in a kitchen who's
suddenly going to become really hot for
her chef. Now this is a Kdrama, so it
was very very slow and very tame
compared to the shows I was used to
watch, you know, like American shows or
whatnot. For some reason, I just
couldn't stop watching. Like I was like,
"My god, this show is so slow and
so like a bit silly
and and somehow I love it. I just do."
So that's how I started with K drama and
until now I've never really stopped.
Right now I'm watching this um Heavenly
Ever After which is a great show. If you
haven't watched it, I highly recommend.
It's with Suko, who is my favorite
actor, who I've seen in real life when I
went to Korea, but I've actually um seen
him at the theater because I paid for
tickets to go see his play even though I
could not understand what was actually
happening in this play, but I was so
close to
it.
Anyhow, from K drama to K-pop, it's
around the same time in 2018 that I
really got into all the K things because
until then I had a few trips to Korea,
but it was really just about seeing the
family. And the only thing I enjoyed
about Korea was like seeing my family
and food, eating, that was it. So, on my
very first trip to Korea, I had heard
K-pop obviously because it was blasting
in the street all the time. At the time,
they had that song everywhere which was
like, "Pick me, pick me, pick me up,
pick me, pick me, something like that."
I don't know. It was super catchy, but
also so annoying. Like, it wouldn't
leave your head every time you would
listen to it, which was all the time.
Before that, I did not know K-pop so
well. When I was in high school, a
friend of mine who really liked K-pop
had sent to me two songs that I had on
my MP3 because yes, I am not as young as
you think. Suncreen. She has sent me Big
Bangs Lies
[Music]
and Strong Baby. And I like those songs
and I was listening to them, but I had
no particular interest. But I started to
relisten to K-pop in 2018
with Okay. Actually, it's a funny story
because one night I was alone at home
and I was just like, hm, I should like
check
K-pop. Yeah, as you do randomly and I
just typed in Google like best K-pop
song or something like that. And what
came up was
BTS My Drop. So I watched that music
video and I was wondering why are those
guys hair so weird but at the same time
I really like the song and the dance
move like yeah. So I clicked on the next
video which
was which again a little bit crazy but
somehow I liked it. And the third one I
clicked on
was and you know when at this beginning
they do like and I don't know I actually
thought that move was so beautiful and
the whole video clip was so beautiful
and I was confused about who was who
because they kept changing the hair once
again but I don't know there was
something about it and since that day I
could not stop listening to it and this
is how my obsession with BTS started and
with BTS came others because BTS was my
first K-pop love but you had also Monex
and Stray Kids and ETSs and many others.
And I'm so glad to have been able to
discover this whole world of K-pop
because I love the songs so much and I
especially love seeing the
performances. Which brings me to my
first K-pop concert which was actually
BTS. It was in 2019 and they were really
really big already and they were playing
at the start of France in in Paris and I
thought okay there's no way I'm actually
going to get any tickets and um I didn't
even try. I didn't want to be
disappointed. Whatever. Um but a girl
from my Korean class, we'll come back to
that. She said that she needed a
recommendation about Paris. And I was
like why? She said, "Oh, I'm going to
the BTS concert." And I was like, "Oh my
god, you're so lucky. like you got
tickets and she's like yeah I actually
have some spare but because I bought
some average ticket and then I managed
to get like much better tickets so I
actually want to sell the average
ticket. I was like wait a minute girl
I'm going to give you all the
recommendation for Paris but you give me
those tickets. Well, she gave me one uh
she gave me one ticket. Um well, I gave
I bought it, but I have a ticket for
BTS. And then when I was in Paris, I was
also looking for a ticket for the other
day because the concert was over 2 days
and I had a ticket for the one day and I
needed a ticket for the other ones, but
I had a budget that had already exploded
just with the, you know, buying the
tickets and flying to France, etc. So, I
had a budget of €50 and I just sent
messages to every single person on every
single platform that would sell ticket.
And I was like, "Hey, my budget is €50.
Um, I'm serious about this. If you have
any ticket, I don't care what it is.
I'll buy it off you." And nobody
responded or like people responded like,
"Girl, this is way too low." Yeah, I
know. But on the day of the concert,
like only a few hours before that,
someone responded was like, "I have
found no other seller." So yeah, you can
have it for €50. So, we met up at the
concert place and I got tickets for the
two days and it was a wonderful,
wonderful, wonderful experience. I did
not know about K-pop concert so much
because I had seen BTS's video clips a
lot and performances on broadcast, you
know, on YouTube and and whatnot, but I
had not seen what it's like at a concert
with all the light. I didn't know really
what the light sticks were for. I was a
really still a newbie to this whole
K-pop world and I saw all the light
sticks in all the colors and everybody
singing and it was like all of the def
France which is a huge stadium like
singing together. It was yeah the show
was like so big like I have been to so
many concert in my life. I used to work
in music festivals but I was blown away
by the actual performance put on with
from the staging the dances the change
of costume the the way everything is
thought to bring you each song's
universe and seeing the boys even though
they were the size of a Lego you know
you know Lego like that size they was so
small but I was seeing them in real and
it was
beautiful and I've seen other any K-pop
concert since, but this BTS, especially
the first time, like the first time I
saw it and these emotions I felt and
this things of seeing something for the
first time live, it really stays in my
heart.
And this whole interest I had in Korean
culture, the Halu uh wave really helped
me in taking learning Korean seriously
because I have two notebooks here. And
this one was from 2016 when I had just
met my family and I was going to go back
with my mom to meet them again so she
could meet her longlost mother. It was
me trying to
learn Oh my god, I haven't seen that in
so long. It's horrible.
So that's me trying to learn the
alphabet, trying to learn the roman
romanization rules. Do not learn the
romanization rule. Do not learn
romanization. Just learn your hunger.
Trust me. And the way I tried to learn
Korean is actually I decided to do a
language exchange from the very
beginning like from I knew nothing. And
I met with my first Korean tenant
partner who was learning English at the
time.
So, it's actually so funny because you
know like he's trying to like teach
me like ah ya or y or you like all of
this and his technique was telling me ah
think stabbed in the front. Ah then oh
you get stabbed in the back. So that's
why like the it go the you know it goes
to the front and then that goes to the
back. Yo, like rapping yo yo
yo like sexy kiss things cute girl. I
remember he was like cute girl. Ooh ooh.
I was like okay that's so
weird. And yeah like that's some of the
like thing he taught me. Like we are
still on my lesson my second lesson of
learning Korean. My second
hour. Um, how much is it? It's It's too
expensive. Yeah, we are still We still a
few pages down, but we still at Omayo.
Yeah, I could not deal with that. I
hadn't looked at this notebook, my first
Korean learning notebook in a very long
time. But we only met for a few weeks
and after that, I forgot everything
because I did not have time to learn
Korean properly and also he went back to
Korea. But this notebook is my first
serious Korean learning notebook and
that's from when I started learning
Korean at the Korean sett. This is 2016.
This is 2019. I started from the
complete beginner level and I went up to
level 4B. So, so from a complete
beginner to a high intermediate level.
And here if we look at these first
lessons that we learning, I mean
obviously we learn hunger first and then
the greetings in summer.
And at this lesson I remember she was
explaining
that come from which is a formal which
means to be there like to exist and ani
is peaceful. supposed to basic basically
stay there peacefully which is why it's
goodbye when the person when you were
leaving and the other person is staying
and you
say from kada which means to leave when
you stay here and the other person is
leaving and so it makes sense it's like
stay here peacefully or leave peacefully
which is something that um ton had never
told me he was just telling me like this
is the one where you leave and this is
the one where you stay and I could never
do the difference between the two of
them And that is why it's good to have a
proper teacher who knows what to say and
what to explain in detail to uh start
learning Korean. So I would recommend to
have a real textbook and a real teacher
either or or both ideally to start
learning Korean. But yeah, that's when I
first started to learn Korean. And it
was a really long journey, but I was
really really happy to learn Korean. And
I'm really happy now to be able to
communicate with my family fully. Like
when I'm there, we speak only Korean.
and I translate for my mom who doesn't
speak Korean because she was born there
but she grew up in France. Learning
Korean, learning to communicate even
though I'm not like 100% fluent. I'm not
100% comfortable speaking Korean. It was
really a massive change in
feeling like I am Korean
myself. And that the thing I never
really felt Korean until actually I
reckon about 2019 when I started
learning the language and starting to
feel familiar with the Korean culture,
starting to feel interested in the
Korean culture and also finding my
Korean name because that's the thing. I
grew up in the French countryside as a
French girl to whom other people were
saying that you don't look like you're
from here. You look Asian. But I didn't
felt Asian. I knew nothing about Asia. I
knew nothing about Korea. I knew I
couldn't identify with with any of this.
And so once I got familiar with the
culture, I got familiar with the
language. I started to really embrace
this side of my identity, I started to
become proud of my Korean heritage. And
another thing that happened in that very
first Korean class that I took was that
um I changed my name, I mean my nickname
because you see I'm called Kim on the
first day of class when they were like
oh if you're called Kim and you add she
at the end she which is sort of the
thing you put after a name to be polite
to people for example Jimn you would say
Jimni you know and with me if you say
the first time when the teacher asked
for my name she was like Kim so it going
to be like Kim
And everybody started laughing. She's
like, "She's called Kimchi." And I had a
wave of like that thing of like when
people were making fun of me at school
for being Asian, they were never
mentioning kimchi. Like no one knew
kimchi at the time, but like just like
oh the shin they were saying in French,
the Chinese girl like it was I was like,
"Oh my god, this is horrible." Like
let's not call me like this. And a lot
of my friends were calling me Kimmy as a
cute nickname. And I was telling the
teacher like, "Oh no, not Kim, Kimmy."
And that how it became Kimishi. And
that's why my name on this channel every
time I speak Korean is Kimmy. Now Park
is not my legal last name, but it was my
mom's name before she was adopted. And
it's my family name, my Korean family
name. So that's why my name is Pakimi.
And when I introduced myself in this
video, I said Pakima. So yeah, that's
full circle from my first trip, my first
Kimchi to now having my first and last
Korean name. and that whole journey of
many K first that brought me to today
being really proud of being Korean. So
yeah, that was my story and that's it
for today. If it was your first time on
this channel, don't forget to subscribe.
I hope you enjoyed the video. If so, let
me know by giving me a thumbs up. Thank
you so much for watching and I'll see
you in the next one.
